chunk_summary: Nutritional strategies for infants with BPD post-discharge focus on high energy intake, fluid restriction, and feeding safety to address increased metabolic demands and comorbid risks like dysphagia and metabolic bone disease.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of caloric density adjustments (e.g., 22-24 kcal/oz) in fluid-restricted infants with BPD.
Recognize the role of monitoring hydration, electrolytes, and metabolic bone health in infants on diuretics or hypercaloric feeds.
Identify feeding strategies to minimize fatigue and ensure swallow safety in BPD infants.
Learn to document and communicate feeding and nutrition plans for community follow-up teams.
key_facts: Infants with BPD require higher energy intake due to increased work of breathing, with caloric density adjustments (e.g., 22-24 kcal/oz).
Avoid formula concentrations above 24 kcal/oz unless under specialist supervision due to risks of free-water imbalance and renal solute load.
Prolonged diuretic use increases risks of electrolyte derangements and metabolic bone disease; monitor phosphorus and ALP levels.
Feeding strategies include shorter, more frequent feeds, paced bottle feeding, and slower-flow nipples to reduce fatigue.
Red flags like increased oxygen needs or choking during feeds require immediate swallow safety evaluation and respiratory stabilization.
reference_quote: Infants with BPD often need higher energy intake because work of breathing increases expenditure, while total fluids may be constrained. (American Academy of Pediatrics, Reference 1)
document_key_topics: Nutritional management in BPD with fluid restriction
Caloric density adjustments and feeding strategies
Monitoring hydration, electrolytes, and bone health in BPD infants
